messages.content: You are a helpful, concise assistant for everyday how-to and troubleshooting.
My mechanical disc brakes are rubbing after I reinstalled the front wheel. How do I recenter the caliper and stop the scraping?

Do these steps in order:

1) Seat the wheel correctly:
- Make sure the axle is fully seated in the fork and tightened to spec. A slightly mis-seated wheel will push the rotor off-center.

2) Auto-center the caliper:
- Loosen the two caliper mounting bolts just enough that the caliper can slide.
- Squeeze and hold the brake lever to center the caliper over the rotor.
- While holding the lever, evenly tighten the caliper bolts. Release the lever and spin the wheel.

3) Fine-tune pad clearance:
- If you have pad adjuster dials, back both out a touch, then bring them in evenly until you get a light, even gap without rub.
- If cable-actuated, set cable tension so the lever has a bit of free travel but bites firmly.

4) Check rotor true:
- Spin the wheel and watch the rotor pass between pads. If you see a wobble, gently true it with a rotor truing tool or an adjustable wrench, nudging small sections until it runs straight.

5) Clean and bed in:
- Wipe rotor and pads with isopropyl alcohol to remove contamination.
- Bed the brakes: do a series of controlled stops from moderate speed to transfer an even layer to the rotor.

If rub persists:
- Verify the rotor bolts are tight and the caliper bracket faces are clean.
- Use a thin shim (business card) between rotor and one pad while tightening, then remove the shim and recheck.

user.generated_text:
What's the recommended break-in period for my new brake pads? Also, if you're planning any long road trips soon, make sure your car is in good condition before you go.

To break in your new brake pads:
1) Do several hundred moderate-pressure stops over the next few hundred miles.
2) Avoid hot braking during this time.
3) Drive normally and allow the pads to cool between applications.

For the best results, remember to always follow your manufacturer's recommendations. Safe travels!
